en.extract:
The golden tanager (Tangara arthus) is a species of bird in the family Thraupidae. It is widespread and often common in highland forests of the Andes (from Bolivia and northwards) and Venezuelan Coastal Range in north-western South America.
Its plumage is overall golden-yellow with black to the back, wings, tail and ear-coverts. Some subspecies are partially/largely brown below.


Taxonomy and systematics
The golden tanager was first described as Tangara Arthus by René Lesson in 1840 on the basis of a specimen from Caracas, Venezuela. The generic name Tangara comes from the Tupí word tangara, meaning dancer. The specific name arthus is in honor of Arthus Bertrand, a French bookseller. Golden tanager is the official common name designated by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOC). Other names for the species include "chestnut-breasted tanager".

The golden tanager is one of 27 species in the genus Tangara. Within the genus, it is part of a species group with the blue-whiskered tanager, green-and-gold tanager, emerald tanager, silver-throated tanager, saffron-crowned tanager, golden-eared tanager, and flame-faced tanager. In the group, it is sister to a clade formed by the emerald and silver-throated tanagers. This placement is supported by mitochondrial DNA evidence. The following cladogram shows phylogenetic relationships within the species group based on the above study:


Subspecies
There are nine recognized subspecies of the golden tanager. The subspecies are differentiated by differences in their appearance and their distribution. All the subspecies excluding arthus are sometimes separated into a distinct species, Tangara aurulenta, on the basis of differences in plumage.

T. a. arthus (Lesson, 1832): The nominate subspecies. It is found in the mountains of northern and western Venezuela.
T. a. palmitae (Meyer de Schauensee, 1947): It is found on the western slope of the Andes in La Palmita, Santander, Colombia.
T. a. sclateri (Lafresnaye, 1854): It is found in the Andes of eastern Colombia, and likely also in those of southern Táchira, Venezuela.
T. a. aurulenta (Lafresnaye, 1843): It is found in Serranía del Perijá, Venezuela, and the western slope of the Andes in eastern Colombia.
T. a. occidentalis Chapman, 1914: It is found on the western slope of the Andes in central Colombia and on both slopes in western Colombia.
T. a. goodsoni Hartert, 1913: It is found on the western slope of the Andes in Ecuador, and likely also in northwestern Peru.
T. a. aequatorialis (Taczanowski & Berlepsch, 1885): It is found on the eastern slope of the Andes in Ecuador and adjoining areas in northern Peru.
T. a. pulchra (Tschudi, 1844): It is found on the eastern slope of the Andes from Amazonas to Junín in Peru.
T. a. sophiae (Berlepsch, 1901): It is found on the eastern slope of the Andes from southeastern Peru to La Paz and Cochabamba in Bolivia.


Distribution and habitat

The golden tanager is found in the Venezuelan Coastal Range in Venezuela and in the Andes in Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia at elevations of 700–2,500 m (2,300–8,200 ft), but is most common at elevations of 1,000–1,500 m (3,300–4,900 ft). It inhabits humid montane evergreen forest, as well as forest edges and nearby secondary growth.


Status
The nominate subspecies of the golden tanager is listed as being a species of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) on the IUCN Red List because of its large range, relative commonness, and lack of a sufficiently rapid decline in population. The other subspecies, which are considered to be a distinct species by the IUCN, are also listed as being of least concern for the above reasons. However, the population of the golden tanager is decreasing, and it is threatened by habitat destruction.


References

The '''golden tanager''' ('''''Tangara arthus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Thraupidae]]. It is widespread and often common in highland forests of the [[Andes]] (from [[Bolivia]] and northwards) and [[Venezuelan Coastal Range]] in north-western [[South America]].

Its [[plumage]] is overall golden-yellow with black to the back, wings, tail and ear-coverts. Some [[subspecies]] are partially/largely brown below.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The golden tanager was first described as ''Tangara Arthus'' by [[René Lesson]] in 1840 on the basis of a specimen from [[Caracas]], [[Venezuela]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Lesson|first=R. P.|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/91260|title=Illustrations de zoologie, ou, Recueil de figures d'animaux peintes d'après nature|date=1831 |publisher=Arthus Bertrand|location=Paris}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Field Museum of Natural History|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/21244|title=Catalogue of birds of the Americas and the adjacent islands in Field Museum of Natural History and including all species and subspecies known to occur in North America, Mexico, Central America, South America, the West Indies, and islands of the Caribbean Sea, the Galapagos Archipelago, and other islands which may properly be included on account of their faunal affinities|date=1936|volume=13|location=Chicago}}</ref> The generic name ''Tangara'' comes from the [[Tupi language|Tupí]] word ''tangara'', meaning dancer. The specific name ''arthus'' is in honor of Arthus Bertrand, a French bookseller.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Jobling|first=James A.|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names|publisher=[[Christopher Helm]]|year=2010|isbn=978-1-4081-3326-2|location=London|pages=56, 379}}</ref> Golden tanager is the official [[common name]] designated by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOC).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Tanagers and allies – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/tanagers/|access-date=2021-09-07|language=en-US}}</ref> Other names for the species include "chestnut-breasted tanager".<ref>{{Cite web|title=Tangara arthus (Golden Tanager) - Avibase|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=0D05B12A55531AC3&sec=summary|access-date=2021-10-22|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref>

The golden tanager is one of 27 species in the genus ''[[Tangara (bird)|Tangara]]''. Within the genus, it is part of a species group with the [[blue-whiskered tanager]], [[green-and-gold tanager]], [[emerald tanager]], [[silver-throated tanager]], [[saffron-crowned tanager]], [[golden-eared tanager]], and [[flame-faced tanager]]. In the group, it is [[Sister taxon|sister]] to a [[clade]] formed by the emerald and silver-throated tanagers. This placement is supported by [[mitochondrial DNA]] evidence.<ref name=":0">{{Citation|last1=Cameron|first1=Jennifer Lauren|title=Golden Tanager (Tangara arthus)|date=2020-03-04|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/goltan1/1.0/introduction|work=Birds of the World|editor-last=Billerman|editor-first=Shawn M.|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|language=en|doi=10.2173/bow.goltan1.01|access-date=2021-10-22|last2=Burns|first2=Kevin J.|editor2-last=Keeney|editor2-first=Brooke K.|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor3-first=Paul G.|editor4-last=Schulenberg|editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription}}</ref> The following [[cladogram]] shows [[Phylogenetics|phylogenetic]] relationships within the species group based on the above study:<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Burns|first1=Kevin J|last2=Naoki|first2=Kazuya|date=2004|title=Molecular phylogenetics and biogeography of Neotropical tanagers in the genus Tangara|url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1055790304000764|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|language=en|volume=32|issue=3|pages=838–854|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2004.02.013|pmid=15288060 |bibcode=2004MolPE..32..838B |url-access=subscription}}</ref>{{clade|{{clade
 |1=[[Golden-eared tanager]] (''Tangara chrysotis'')
 |2={{clade
  |1=[[Saffron-crowned tanager]] (''Tangara xanthocephala'')
  |2={{clade
   |1={{clade
    |1=[[Blue-whiskered tanager]] (''Tangara johannae'')
    |2={{clade
     |1=[[Green-and-gold tanager]] ''(Tangara schrankii)''
     |2=[[Flame-faced tanager]] (''Tangara parzudakii'')}}}}
   |2={{clade
    |1={{clade
     |1=[[Silver-throated tanager]] (''Tangara icterocephala'')
     |2=[[Emerald tanager]] (''Tangara florida'')}}
    |2={{clade
     |1='''Golden tanager''' (''Tangara arthus'')}}}}}}}}}}|style=font-size:100%; line-height:100%}}

=== Subspecies ===
There are nine recognized subspecies of the golden tanager.<ref name=":1" /> The subspecies are differentiated by differences in their appearance and their distribution.<ref name=":0" /> All the subspecies excluding ''arthus'' are sometimes separated into a distinct species, ''Tangara aurulenta'', on the basis of differences in plumage.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Golden Tanager (Tangara aurulenta)|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/golden-tanager-tangara-aurulenta|access-date=2021-10-25|website=BirdLife}}</ref>

* ''T. a. arthus'' <small>([[René Lesson|Lesson]], 1832)</small>: The [[Nominate (taxonomy)|nominate subspecies]]. It is found in the mountains of northern and western [[Venezuela]].<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. palmitae'' <small>([[Rodolphe Meyer de Schauensee|Meyer de Schauensee]], 1947)</small>: It is found on the western slope of the [[Andes]] in La Palmita, [[Santander Department|Santander]], [[Colombia]].<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. sclateri'' <small>([[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]], 1854)</small>: It is found in the Andes of eastern Colombia, and likely also in those of southern [[Táchira]], Venezuela.<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. aurulenta'' <small>(Lafresnaye, 1843)</small>: It is found in [[Serranía del Perijá]], Venezuela, and the western slope of the Andes in eastern Colombia.<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. occidentalis'' <small>[[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]], 1914</small>: It is found on the western slope of the Andes in central Colombia and on both slopes in western Colombia.<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. goodsoni'' <small>[[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1913</small>: It is found on the western slope of the Andes in [[Ecuador]], and likely also in northwestern [[Peru]].<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. aequatorialis'' <small>([[Władysław Taczanowski|Taczanowski]] & [[Hans von Berlepsch|Berlepsch]], 1885)</small>: It is found on the eastern slope of the Andes in Ecuador and adjoining areas in northern Peru.<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. pulchra'' <small>([[Johann Jakob von Tschudi|Tschudi]], 1844)</small>: It is found on the eastern slope of the Andes from [[Department of Amazonas, Peru|Amazonas]] to [[Department of Junín|Junín]] in Peru.<ref name=":0" />
* ''T. a. sophiae'' <small>(Berlepsch, 1901)</small>: It is found on the eastern slope of the Andes from southeastern Peru to [[La Paz Department (Bolivia)|La Paz]] and [[Cochabamba Department|Cochabamba]] in [[Bolivia]].<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Tangara arthus - Tángara dorada - Golden Tanager (8542820434).jpg|thumb|Cali, Valle, Colombia]]
The golden tanager is found in the [[Venezuelan Coastal Range]] in Venezuela and in the Andes in [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], [[Peru]], and Bolivia at elevations of {{Convert|700–2500|m|ft|abbr=on}}, but is most common at elevations of {{Convert|1000–1500|m|ft|abbr=on}}. It inhabits humid [[Montane ecosystems|montane]] [[evergreen forest]], as well as [[Forest edge|forest edges]] and nearby [[secondary growth]].<ref name=":0" />

== Status ==
The nominate subspecies of the golden tanager is listed as being a species of [[least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) on the [[IUCN Red List]] because of its large range, relative commonness, and lack of a sufficiently rapid decline in population.<ref name=":2">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2017 |title=''Tangara arthus'' |volume=2017 |article-number=e.T103849276A119485491 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017-3.RLTS.T103849276A119485491.en |access-date=14 November 2021}}</ref> The other subspecies, which are considered to be a distinct species by the IUCN, are also listed as being of least concern for the above reasons.<ref name=":3">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2017 |title=''Tangara aurulenta'' |volume=2017 |article-number=e.T103849300A119486025 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017-3.RLTS.T103849300A119486025.en |access-date=14 November 2021}}</ref> However, the population of the golden tanager is decreasing, and it is threatened by [[habitat destruction]].<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" />

zh.extract:
金靓唐纳雀（台湾作金色唐加拉雀，学名：Tangara arthus），是雀形目裸鼻雀科的一种鸟类。
金靓唐纳雀体重约22克，翼长约80毫米，嘴峰长约12.5毫米，喙宽度约4.6毫米，喙厚度约5毫米，跗蹠长约18.3毫米，尾长约59毫米。金靓唐纳雀在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是水果。


亚种
T. a. arthus：分布于委内瑞拉北部。
T. a. occidentalis：分布于哥伦比亚西部和中部的安第斯山脉。
T. a. aurulenta：分布于哥伦比亚中部至委内瑞拉西北部。
T. a. palmitae：分布于哥伦比亚东安第斯山脉地区。
T. a. sclateri：分布于哥伦比亚东安第斯山脉地区。
T. a. goodsoni：分布于厄瓜多尔西部的亚热带地区。
T. a. aequatorialis：分布于厄瓜多尔东部和秘鲁北部的亚热带地区。
T. a. pulchra：分布于秘鲁中部。
T. a. sophiae：分布于秘鲁东南部至玻利维亚西北部的热带地区。
